video_title: Underworld Confessions Untold Stories From Ex-Mafia Member The Brooklyn Faction Was Ruthless
notes: SEED DATA — replace the transcript array with real transcript chunks from the extension's console output. Refine human_labels after running the eval to see what the AI actually produces. The labels below are best-guess starting points based on typical mafia-interview content patterns.

transcript.text: [Music]
Back to uh the neighborhood the Brooklyn faction was you know they were ruthless absolutely ruthless and we knew it
because when you're growing up in that environment you see things as a kid that you shouldn't see and it shapes who you become
the Brooklyn crew they had a different mentality than the other boroughs they didn't care about the rules as much they just wanted to get things done
I remember one time there was a situation where a guy owed money and he thought he could just you know hide from it and that's not how it works
so they sent some guys over to his place of business and within 24 hours the money was there I mean that's just how fast things moved
wow that's incredible so they really had that kind of reach even back then
oh absolutely and it wasn't just Brooklyn they had connections everywhere I mean we're talking about a network that extended into New Jersey into Connecticut
the hierarchy was very clear you had the boss then you had the underboss the consigliere and then the captains each captain had his own crew
right right or go to a meeting or something yeah totally
exactly and each crew had specific responsibilities some handled the unions some handled the construction some handled the gambling operations
so let me ask you about something that a lot of people are curious about how did the initiation process work what was that ceremony like
so the initiation it's called getting made and it's a very serious thing you don't just walk in and say hey I want to be a member there's a whole process
first you have to be proposed by a made member someone who vouches for you with their life and I mean that literally if you screw up the guy who proposed you pays for it
then there's a period where they watch you they test you they see how you handle yourself under pressure do you keep your mouth shut do you follow orders
that's crazy man that's absolutely insane when you think about it
and then the actual ceremony it's very traditional you sit at a table with the boss and the other made members they prick your finger they put a picture of a saint in your hand
and they light it on fire and you swear an oath that you will live and die by the rules of this thing of ours and that's it you're in for life
there's no retirement plan there's no two weeks notice once you're in you're in until you're in the ground or you're in prison
so speaking of prison because you did spend time behind bars what was that experience like for a made member going into the prison system
prison for a made guy is different than for a regular inmate when you walk in people know who you are there's a certain respect that comes with the name
but it's also dangerous because there are people in there who want to make a name for themselves and taking out a mob guy that's a big scalp
so you always had to watch your back you always had to be aware of your surroundings and you had to have your people around you
hey guys before we continue I want to tell you about this channel if you haven't already make sure you like and subscribe hit that bell notification so you don't miss any of our episodes
we've also got merch available check the link in the description we've got t-shirts hoodies all kinds of stuff alright back to the conversation
so Frank when you got out of prison what was re-entering society like after being in that world for so long
re-entering society that's the hardest part because the world moves on without you technology changed everything changed my kids grew up while I was inside
and you can't just go back to the life because you're on parole they're watching everything you do where you go who you talk to
it's like being a fish out of water you know everything you knew how to do everything you were good at you can't do anymore you gotta find a new way
yeah yeah that makes total sense man wow just wow
and the hardest thing is the loneliness because all those guys you thought were your brothers when you're no longer useful they disappear
that's the biggest lesson I learned loyalty in that world is conditional it lasts exactly as long as you're useful and not one second longer
so now I try to use my experience to help kids stay out of that life I go to schools I talk to community groups I tell them the truth
I tell them there's no glory in it there's no movie ending you either end up dead or in prison and if you're lucky enough to get out like I did you've lost everything
the neighborhood has changed too a lot of the old places are gone the bakeries the social clubs the places where we used to hang out it's all been gentrified
you know what it reminds me of actually my grandmother used to make this incredible pasta and we'd all sit around on Sundays and that was the one day where everything was normal
it was like the mob life didn't exist on Sundays at grandma's house we were just a family eating pasta and that's what I miss the most
speaking of food though I was at this restaurant last week and the waiter recognized me from the show and he was like oh my god are you Frank Gioia
and I'm like yeah that's me and he's like can I get a photo and that's just so surreal to me because 30 years ago nobody wanted a photo with a mob guy
but going back to the Brooklyn crew specifically there was one incident that really showed how brutal they could be and this is something that's never been told before
there was a meeting called and everyone thought it was a regular sit-down but what nobody knew was that the boss had already made the decision someone at that table wasn't leaving
and the way it went down was so cold so calculated that even the other guys at the table were shaken by it and these were hardened guys who had seen everything
that's what separated the Brooklyn faction from the others they didn't do things half-hearted when they made a decision they followed through completely
and the message it sent was clear if you cross us there's no conversation there's no second chance there's just consequences
and that reputation is what kept people in line for decades because everyone knew the stories everyone knew what happened if you stepped out of line
that's wild man I got chills just hearing that that's absolutely wild
but you know what I think the most important thing people should understand is that this life it destroys everyone it touches not just the guys in it but their families their kids
my kids grew up without a father my wife had to raise them alone and that's on me I can't blame anyone else for that choice
so when kids come up to me and say they think the mob life is cool I tell them go talk to my daughter go ask her if she thinks it was cool growing up visiting her dad in prison
that usually shuts them up pretty quick because the reality is nothing like what you see in the movies
alright Frank this has been an incredible conversation thank you so much for sharing these stories with us really appreciate you being so open
of course man I appreciate you having me on it's important that these stories get told so people understand what this life is really about
guys that's going to do it for today's episode make sure you hit that subscribe button if you haven't already leave a comment down below let us know what you thought
and if you want to see more content like this check out our Patreon link in the description we've got exclusive episodes and behind the scenes content
also follow us on Instagram and Twitter at the links below we post clips and updates about upcoming guests
Frank where can people find you what are you working on these days where can they follow you
you can find me on Instagram at Frank Gioia official and I've got a book coming out later this year so stay tuned for that
[Music]
